seoTitle: How to Use Requirements in Jira
metaDescription: Learn how to track and manage requirements in Jira so they stay linked to the stories and tasks built to fulfill them.
intro: Keeping requirements tied to the work that fulfills them helps teams avoid scope drift and makes it easier to trace why a feature exists. This guide covers how to manage requirements inside Jira and link them to the related development work.

steps.heading: Decide how requirements will be tracked
steps.body: Requirements can live as their own issue type, as linked Confluence documentation, or as detailed descriptions on epics, depending on how your team works.

steps.heading: Create the requirement
steps.body: Document the requirement clearly, whether as a Jira issue or a linked page, including enough detail for the team to understand what needs to be delivered.

steps.heading: Link requirements to stories or tasks
steps.body: Connect the requirement to the specific stories or tasks that will fulfill it, using Jira's issue linking feature.

steps.heading: Track progress against the requirement
steps.body: As linked work moves through the pipeline, use the requirement's linked issues to see how much of it has been completed.

steps.heading: Review and update as needed
steps.body: Revisit requirements periodically to make sure they still reflect what's needed, updating linked issues if scope changes.

faq.question: Does Jira have a dedicated requirements issue type?
faq.answer: Not always by default, so many teams use epics, a custom issue type, or linked Confluence pages to capture requirements.

faq.question: How do I trace which stories fulfill a requirement?
faq.answer: Use Jira's issue linking feature to connect stories or tasks directly to the requirement they support.

faq.question: Can I track requirement completion visually?
faq.answer: Yes, if requirements are structured as epics or linked issues, you can often see a progress rollup of the linked work.

faq.question: Who typically owns requirements in Jira?
faq.answer: This varies by team, but product managers or business analysts commonly own writing and maintaining requirements.
